name: Dead Bug
goal: Build deep abdominal control and learn to hold a stable spine under load
description: Dead bug variation with a yoga block or foam roller held between your knees and elbows. The prop forces you to engage your inner thighs and arms symmetrically so you can't cheat.
howTo: Lie on your back with knees bent to 90 degrees and arms reaching to the ceiling. Place a yoga block or small foam roller between your knees and your elbows — keep light pressure on it throughout. Imprint your lower back gently into the floor. Slowly lower one arm overhead and the opposite leg toward the floor, exhaling all the way down. Only go as far as you can without your lower back lifting. Return and switch sides. The block should never drop or get crushed.

name: Rises with Ball
goal: Train an even, straight rise through demi-pointe and a controlled roll-down, avoiding sickling or rolling in
description: Slow rises in parallel with a small ball (tennis ball or Pilates ball) held between your ankles. The ball keeps your ankles tracking straight and prevents them from rolling in or out.
howTo: Stand in parallel with a small ball held lightly between your inner ankles. Rise slowly through all five toes, pressing evenly through the ball of each foot. Pause at the top — check that your ankles aren't rolling inward. Roll down just as slowly, keeping the ball in place and the weight even across both feet. Focus on the little toe side of the foot staying connected to the floor as you roll up, and the heel lowering last on the way down.

name: Reverse Sit-Up
goal: Strengthen the muscles along the back of the spine needed for arabesque and back port de bras
description: Prone back extension, using only the muscles of the back to lift the upper body. No hands pressing into the floor.
howTo: Lie face down with your arms long by your sides and legs hip-width apart. Engage your outer hips and inner thighs lightly. On an exhale, lift your head, chest, and arms off the floor as a single unit — think of reaching your hands toward your feet rather than simply arching back. Hold for a breath, then lower slowly. Keep your neck long and in line with your spine. If it helps, imagine there's a small gap under your lower ribs that you want to maintain rather than collapse into the floor.

name: Cobra to Second Arabesque
goal: Build back strength and train the feeling of length in second arabesque
description: Push up into a seal or cobra position, then extend one arm out to the side into the line of second arabesque. Combines back extension strength with arabesque arm coordination.
howTo: Lie face down with hands under your shoulders. Press up into a seal or cobra — how high you go depends on your back flexibility, but keep your hips on or close to the floor. Once stable, extend one arm out to the side and slightly forward in the line of second arabesque, reaching long through the fingertips. Feel the diagonal from your extended arm through your spine. Hold, then lower down with control and switch arms. Keep your shoulders away from your ears throughout.

name: Bird Dog
goal: Train the trunk to stay centred while the limbs move — directly transfers to balance and arabesque
description: From all-fours, extend one arm and the opposite leg while keeping the torso completely still. The goal is not to shift your weight at all as you move.
howTo: Start on all-fours with your wrists under your shoulders and knees under your hips. Before moving, find your neutral spine and draw your lower belly in lightly. Extend one arm forward and the opposite leg back simultaneously — slowly. The trunk should not rotate, dip to one side, or shift weight into the grounded knee. Imagine balancing a glass of water on your lower back: it shouldn't spill. Hold for 3 to 5 seconds, return with control, and switch. Go slowly enough that you can feel any sway and stop it.

name: Aeroplanes
goal: Build single-leg ankle stability and the arabesque hip hinge pattern
description: Stand on one leg and slowly hinge your whole torso forward until it's parallel to the floor, while the free leg extends behind you — moving between parallel retire height and arabesque. Works the standing ankle and trains the arabesque shape under control.
howTo: Stand on one leg in a small parallel releve or flat. Softly bend the standing knee. Slowly hinge forward from the hip, letting the free leg lift behind you as a counterbalance — not as the focus. Move the free leg between the height of a parallel retire (roughly hip height) and an arabesque line as your torso lowers toward parallel with the floor. Keep your hips level and your standing ankle absolutely still — no rolling in or out. Pause at the lowest point, then use your glute and hamstring to hinge back up. Avoid rushing.
